name: White Oak

harvesting.viabilityTest: Float test: viable acorns sink in water.

dormancySecret.details: Cold stratification in fridge for 30-60 days.

longTermCare.year1: Protect from rodents using wire mesh.
longTermCare.year5: Prune lower branches to encourage a strong central leader.

funFact: White oak wood is watertight and used to make whiskey barrels.

name: Sugar Maple

harvesting.viabilityTest: Cut test: the inside should be firm and green/white.

dormancySecret.details: Cold stratification in fridge for 60-90 days.

longTermCare.year1: Ensure consistent moisture; they are sensitive to drought.
longTermCare.year5: Monitor for aphids and apply horticultural oil if needed.

funFact: It takes about 40 gallons of sap to make one gallon of maple syrup.

name: Elderberry
identification: Tiny, dark purple to black berries
Small, hard seeds inside the berry

harvesting.viabilityTest: Berries should be plump and dark; avoid green ones.

dormancySecret.details: Warm stratification for 60 days, then cold for 90 days.

longTermCare.year1: Weed aggressively around the base to reduce competition.
longTermCare.year5: Coppice (cut back to the ground) to encourage vigorous new fruiting canes.

funFact: The stems are hollow and were historically used to make flutes.

name: Weeping Willow
identification: Tiny seeds attached to white, cottony fluff

harvesting.viabilityTest: Seeds must be sown immediately; they lose viability in days.

dormancySecret.details: Sow immediately on moist soil surface; needs light to germinate.

longTermCare.year1: Keep soil constantly wet; they thrive in waterlogged conditions.
longTermCare.year5: Inspect for willow blight and prune affected weeping branches.

funFact: Aspirin was originally derived from the bark of willow trees.

name: American Sycamore
identification: Breaks apart into tiny, tufted seeds
Tan to brown color when mature

harvesting.viabilityTest: Seed balls should be brown and easily break apart.

dormancySecret.details: Cold stratification in fridge for 30-45 days.

longTermCare.year1: Provide ample space; they grow incredibly fast.
longTermCare.year5: Monitor for anthracnose disease, especially in wet springs.

funFact: It can grow to have the largest trunk diameter of any Eastern US hardwood.

name: Giant Sequoia
identification: Light brown with a papery wing

harvesting.viabilityTest: Cones must be dried to open; seeds should be plump, not flat.

dormancySecret.details: Cold stratification in fridge for 30-60 days.

longTermCare.year1: Protect from direct, intense afternoon sun to prevent scorching.
longTermCare.year5: Ensure excellent drainage; they are highly susceptible to root rot.

funFact: Their thick bark is fire-resistant, and they actually rely on fire to open their cones.

name: Bristlecone Pine
identification: Found in dark purple, bristle-tipped cones

dormancySecret.details: Cold stratification in fridge for 30 days.

longTermCare.year1: Plant in extremely well-draining, rocky soil.
longTermCare.year5: Do not overwater or fertilize; they thrive in harsh, nutrient-poor conditions.

funFact: The oldest known living non-clonal organism on Earth is a Bristlecone Pine named Methuselah, over 4,800 years old.

name: Ginkgo Biloba
identification: Emits a foul, rancid butter smell when ripe

harvesting.viabilityTest: Clean off the fleshy coat; the hard nut should feel heavy.

dormancySecret.details: Warm stratification for 60 days, then cold for 60 days.

longTermCare.year1: Protect from late spring frosts which can kill new growth.
longTermCare.year5: Minimal care needed; they are incredibly resilient to pollution and pests.

funFact: It is a 'living fossil', with no close living relatives, having survived the Hiroshima atomic blast.

name: Baobab
identification: Embedded in a dry, powdery pulp

harvesting.viabilityTest: Seeds should rattle inside the hard pod when shaken.

dormancySecret.details: Nick the hard shell or soak in hot water for 24 hours.

longTermCare.year1: Keep warm and slightly dry; highly susceptible to damping off.
longTermCare.year5: Repot into a larger container if growing indoors, as the taproot is massive.

funFact: Their massive trunks can store thousands of gallons of water to survive harsh droughts.

name: Dragon Blood Tree
identification: Turn from green to black when ripe

harvesting.viabilityTest: Berries must be fully black; clean pulp immediately.

dormancySecret.details: Lightly sand the seed coat and soak in warm water for 24 hours.

longTermCare.year1: Requires high humidity but very well-draining soil.
longTermCare.year5: Ensure it receives full, intense sun to develop its iconic umbrella shape.

funFact: It bleeds a dark red resin, known as 'dragon's blood', used in ancient medicines and dyes.
